title: Speaking Idioms & Register
description: Natural spoken idioms for IELTS Speaking Part 2-3 that sound like real conversation rather than memorised phrases, each flagged for register (informal / neutral) so learners know when it fits.

cards.definition: something that has both a positive and a negative side at the same time; neutral register, good for a balanced opinion.
cards.example: Social media is a bit of a double-edged sword — it keeps you connected, but it can also be a huge distraction.

cards.definition: extremely happy or delighted about something; informal, common when talking about good news.
cards.example: When I got my exam results, I was over the moon.

cards.definition: to start studying seriously, especially before an exam; informal.
cards.example: I've got a test next week, so I really need to hit the books this weekend.

cards.definition: when considering the more distant future rather than the present moment; neutral, useful in Part 3 discussion.
cards.example: Cycling to work might take longer, but in the long run it's better for your health and your wallet.

cards.word: off the top of my head
cards.definition: said before giving an answer based on memory alone, without checking facts; informal, useful when you're not fully sure.
cards.example: Off the top of my head, I'd say most people in my city spend at least an hour a day online.

cards.definition: used to introduce a frank or personal opinion; neutral-to-informal, very natural in spoken answers.
cards.example: To be honest, I've never been a big fan of reality TV shows.

cards.word: at the end of the day
cards.definition: used to introduce what really matters after considering everything; neutral, common when giving a final opinion.
cards.example: There are pros and cons to living in a big city, but at the end of the day, it comes down to personal preference.

cards.definition: a general principle based on practical experience rather than an exact rule; neutral.
cards.example: As a rule of thumb, the earlier you book flights, the cheaper they tend to be.

cards.definition: very rarely; informal, useful when answering frequency questions.
cards.example: I only eat fast food once in a blue moon, maybe a couple of times a year.

cards.definition: to learn how to do something after practising it; informal.
cards.example: It took me a while, but I eventually got the hang of using chopsticks.

cards.definition: something very easy to do; informal.
cards.example: The listening section wasn't too bad, but the writing task was definitely not a piece of cake.

cards.definition: feeling slightly unwell; informal, softer than saying you're ill.
cards.example: I've been feeling a bit under the weather, so I stayed home from work today.

cards.definition: to like someone and get along well from the moment you meet them; informal.
cards.example: We hit it off straight away when we met at university.

cards.definition: to have just enough money to cover basic living costs; neutral-to-informal.
cards.example: With rent prices rising, a lot of young people are struggling to make ends meet.

cards.definition: to watch or monitor something carefully; neutral, common in everyday spoken English.
cards.example: My neighbour asked me to keep an eye on her flat while she was away.

cards.definition: to agree with someone or understand a situation in the same way; neutral, useful when discussing teamwork.
cards.example: Before we started the project, we made sure everyone was on the same page.

cards.definition: to think creatively rather than in a conventional way; neutral, common when discussing problem-solving.
cards.example: The company encourages employees to think outside the box when solving customer problems.

cards.word: take something with a pinch of salt
cards.definition: to not fully believe something because it might not be completely accurate; neutral, useful when discussing the media.
cards.example: You should take a lot of what you read online with a pinch of salt.

cards.definition: something that initially seems bad but turns out to have a good result; neutral, works well when reflecting on a personal experience.
cards.example: Losing that job was actually a blessing in disguise — it pushed me to start my own business.

cards.definition: to stay up late working or studying; informal.
cards.example: I had to burn the midnight oil to finish my dissertation on time.

cards.definition: to force yourself to do something difficult or unpleasant that you've been avoiding; informal.
cards.example: I finally bit the bullet and booked my driving test.

cards.definition: to say or do something to relieve tension or awkwardness, especially when people meet for the first time; neutral.
cards.example: The teacher asked us a few fun questions to break the ice on the first day of class.

cards.definition: to do something in the cheapest or easiest way, often lowering quality; neutral, useful when discussing standards.
cards.example: Some builders cut corners to finish a job faster, which can affect safety.

cards.definition: to suddenly feel too nervous to do something you had planned; informal.
cards.example: She almost cancelled her trip abroad because she got cold feet at the last minute.

cards.definition: to make more effort than is expected; neutral, common when talking about good service or dedication.
cards.example: Our teacher always goes the extra mile to help students who are struggling.

cards.word: have a lot on your plate
cards.definition: to have many tasks or responsibilities to deal with; informal.
cards.example: I can't take on another project right now — I've already got a lot on my plate.

cards.word: hit the nail on the head
cards.definition: to describe exactly what is causing a problem or situation; informal.
cards.example: You really hit the nail on the head when you said the main issue is a lack of communication.

cards.definition: to hope that something will happen the way you want; informal.
cards.example: I've got my job interview tomorrow, so keep your fingers crossed for me.

cards.word: kill two birds with one stone
cards.definition: to achieve two things by doing a single action; neutral, common when describing efficient solutions.
cards.example: I walked to the shop and visited my friend on the way, killing two birds with one stone.

cards.definition: to learn how to do a new job or task; informal.
cards.example: It took me about a month to learn the ropes at my new job.

cards.definition: to release stress or strong emotion, often through physical activity; informal.
cards.example: After a stressful week, I like to go for a run just to let off steam.

cards.definition: to become so absorbed in something that you forget how much time has passed; neutral.
cards.example: I was so into the book that I completely lost track of time.

cards.definition: used to sum up a longer explanation quickly; informal, useful when a Part 2 answer risks running too long.
cards.example: To make a long story short, we missed the flight and had to book another one.

cards.definition: to lose an opportunity by acting too slowly; informal.
cards.example: By the time I applied for the scholarship, I'd already missed the boat.

cards.definition: to notice a small detail or a change in a situation; neutral.
cards.example: My mum always picks up on it when something's bothering me.

cards.definition: to stay awake all night to finish work or studying; informal, common among students.
cards.example: I had to pull an all-nighter to get my essay finished before the deadline.

cards.definition: to delay doing something, often because you don't want to do it; neutral-to-informal.
cards.example: I kept putting off my dentist appointment until the toothache got unbearable.

cards.definition: to think very hard in order to remember or solve something; informal.
cards.example: I racked my brains trying to remember where I'd left my keys.

cards.definition: to agree with someone about something, often used in the negative; neutral.
cards.example: My brother and I don't always see eye to eye on politics.

cards.definition: to avoid making a decision or taking a side in an argument; neutral, useful when discussing opinions.
cards.example: A lot of people tend to sit on the fence when it comes to controversial topics.

cards.definition: to say honestly what you think, even if it might not please others; neutral.
cards.example: I've always admired people who aren't afraid to speak their mind.

cards.definition: to continue to hold your position or opinion despite pressure to change it; informal.
cards.example: Even though her friends disagreed, she stuck to her guns and studied medicine anyway.

cards.definition: to relax and not do too much, especially when tired or unwell; informal.
cards.example: After the exams finished, I just wanted to take it easy for a few days.

cards.definition: to make brief contact with someone to check how things are going; neutral, common in work contexts.
cards.example: I try to touch base with my old university friends at least once a month.

cards.definition: to think carefully about the different choices available before deciding; neutral.
cards.example: Before choosing a university, it's worth weighing up your options carefully.

cards.definition: used to say that you forgot to do or remember something; informal.
cards.example: I meant to call her back, but it completely slipped my mind.

cards.definition: used to say that something sounds simple but is actually difficult to achieve; neutral, useful for a balanced Part 3 answer.
cards.example: Eating healthily sounds simple, but with a busy schedule it's easier said than done.

cards.definition: used to say that what people do matters more than what they say; neutral, common when discussing values or leadership.
cards.example: My grandfather never gave long speeches, but actions speak louder than words, and he was always the first to help.

cards.definition: used to introduce the specific topic you are about to discuss; neutral, a natural way to narrow down an answer.
cards.example: When it comes to saving money, young people today face very different challenges than my parents did.

cards.definition: not yet decided or certain; neutral, useful when talking about future plans.
cards.example: My plans for next year are still a bit up in the air.
